meta.title: S124 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes‑Benz E‑Class S124 engine — OM60x diesel, M102, M103, M104, M111, AMG M104. Real UK costs, classic‑car reliability, and honest buying advice. Get free quotes from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes‑Benz E‑Class S124 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned S124 engine typically costs £1,200–£14,000 supply only, depending on engine — the volume OM602 diesel sits at £1,500–£2,800, while the rare AMG M104.992 commands £8,000–£14,000. Fitting adds £650–£1,500. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes‑Benz E‑Class S124 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM603 3.0‑litre six‑cylinder diesel (300 TD) is widely regarded as one of the most durable engines Mercedes ever built — a cast‑iron block, mechanical injection, and a documented 500,000‑mile lifespan. The OM602 five‑cylinder is a close second, with many examples exceeding 300,000 miles.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes‑Benz E‑Class S124?

acceptedAnswer.text: Injector seal and glow plug failure on the OM60x diesel engines, head gasket and oil leaks on the M103/M104 petrol sixes, carburettor or KE‑Jetronic issues on the M102 four‑cylinder, and — unrelated to the engine — self‑levelling rear suspension wear on high‑mileage estate examples.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes‑Benz E‑Class S124 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — the S124 is one of the most robust cars ever built, provided you choose the right engine. The OM60x diesel series is legendary for durability. The M103 petrol is good but needs head gasket attention, while the M104 is more powerful but suffers from biodegradable wiring harness degradation. The AMG is a collector's item — buy with expert advice.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes‑Benz E‑Class S124 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but avoid the early carburettor M102 (200 TE) unless you're comfortable with classic‑car carburettor maintenance. Also be cautious with the M104 3.0 24‑valve without documented wiring harness replacement — the biodegradable looms can cause electrical nightmares.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my Mercedes‑Benz E‑Class S124 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Hard starting (OM60x injector seals), coolant loss or mayonnaise in the oil (M103/M104 head gasket), rough idle (M102 carburettor/KE‑Jetronic), or any major mechanical knock are the key warning signs. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes‑Benz E‑Class S124 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's body condition. A £2,000–£4,000 OM603 recon against a £3,000 S124 is 67–133% of the car's value — borderline. Against a well‑kept £8,000+ example, the same recon drops to 25–50% — safe. Body condition is the deciding factor on classic S124s, more than engine condition.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes‑Benz E‑Class S124 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM60x diesel series can exceed 500,000 miles with proper servicing — many examples are still on their original engines. The M103 and M104 petrol sixes can comfortably reach 250,000 miles with regular maintenance, though head gasket attention is a recurring need. The AMG M104.992 is more variable due to its rarity and performance nature.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the S124 estate and the W124 saloon?

acceptedAnswer.text: Both are W124‑generation cars, sharing the same chassis and engine line‑up. The S124 is the 5‑door estate/Shooting Brake (1985–1996, this page), while the W124 is the 4‑door saloon (1985–1995). The S124 has a longer wheelbase and self‑levelling rear suspension to handle load. Costs vary slightly due to suspension complexity — see each generation's own page for specifics.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes‑Benz E‑Class S124 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on most engines. Tell us your variant (e.g., 300 TD, E 220 T, E 36 AMG) and approximate registration year (pre‑ or post‑1993) and we'll identify it — 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Mercedes‑Benz E‑Class S124 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the S124 estate — from the 72hp OM601 diesel to the 220hp M104 six‑cylinder and the rare AMG 3.6. Real UK costs, classic‑car running realities, and the bulletproof diesel‑six legend that defines this generation. Backed by real UK S124 enquiries in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes‑Benz E‑Class S124 Engine Codes — The Complete Database

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: OM60x diesel series: 95% confidence — these are widely regarded as among the most durable diesel engines ever built. M102/M103/M104 petrol: 85% confidence — robust but with known head gasket and oil leak issues on early examples. M111: 85% confidence — one of Mercedes' most durable modern four‑cylinders. All ratings are PROVISIONAL — pending dedicated Engine Pages for each family.

overview.intro: The S124 is the estate bodystyle of the Mercedes‑Benz W124 range, launched in October 1985 as the load‑lugging counterpart to the executive saloon. It shared the W124's advanced engineering — including one of the lowest drag coefficients of its era, multi‑link rear suspension, and a modular engine family spanning four, five and six‑cylinder configurations. A major 1993 model‑year update brought the E‑Class naming convention and the M111 and M104 engine families, making this generation a genuine two‑era classic under one body code. The S124 remains one of the most cherished estate cars ever built, prized for its legendary build quality and the bulletproof diesel engines that routinely exceed 300,000 miles.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes‑Benz W124 • Predecessor: S123 • Successor: S210 • Sibling body styles in this generation: W124 Saloon, C124 Coupé, A124 Cabriolet.
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The W124 range (saloon and estate combined) generated real UK engine enquiries in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED], with the OM603 diesel and M104 petrol commanding the strongest specialist interest among classic Mercedes enthusiasts.

items.engine: OM603 3.0‑litre six‑cylinder diesel (300 TD, 110hp / 300 TD Turbo, 143–147hp)
items.quote: The cast‑iron block, pre‑combustion chamber diesel six that built Mercedes' reputation. No turbo on early cars, no DPF, no EGR — just mechanical fuel injection and a documented 500,000‑mile lifespan. Head gasket issues are virtually unheard of with proper coolant maintenance.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want the most durable diesel engine Mercedes ever built.

items.quote: The multi‑valve evolution of the M103 — smoother, more powerful, and with a genuine 220hp that transforms the S124 into a rapid estate. The M104 is a stout motor that benefits from a well‑documented maintenance regime.
items.whoItsFor: enthusiasts who want six‑cylinder refinement and genuine performance.

items.quote: The carburettor is the weak link — early cars suffer from cold‑start and idle issues as the Stromberg unit ages. The later KE‑Jetronic injected versions are more reliable but still not as durable as the M111 that replaced them.
items.whoItsFor: buyers on a tight budget who are comfortable with classic‑car carburettor maintenance.

items.quote: The rarest S124 engine — a 3.6‑litre hand‑built AMG unit with 272hp. Parts are scarce, specialist knowledge is limited, and a full rebuild is a five‑figure bill.
items.whoItsFor: collectors and AMG enthusiasts with deep pockets.

items.engine: OM602 2.5‑litre five‑cylinder diesel (250 TD, 90hp / 250 TD Turbo, 126hp)
items.quote: The five‑cylinder diesel is the Goldilocks engine — more refined than the four, more economical than the six, and the turbo version delivers 126hp with genuine usability. The OM602 is bulletproof and parts remain widely available.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want diesel durability without the six‑cylinder's running costs.

items.quote: The post‑1993 M111 is one of Mercedes' most durable modern four‑cylinders — 16 valves, 150hp, and none of the carburettor or early KE‑Jetronic complications. It's the sensible choice in the petrol range, often overlooked in favour of the sixes.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want modern petrol reliability without the six‑cylinder's fuel consumption.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Slow but Sturdy — the entry‑level diesel
Turbo Six — the best diesel estate
Early Petrol — carburettor is the weak link

economicsRule.title: The S124 rule
economicsRule.text: A £2,000–£4,000 OM603 recon against a £3,000–£8,000 S124 sits at 25–133% of the car's value — on a high‑mileage £3,000 example, that brushes the 70% Mercedes‑Benz threshold. Against a well‑kept £8,000+ example, the same recon drops to 25–50% — a routine repair decision, not a financial one. Value the car and its body condition first.

problems.title: OM60x Diesel Injector Seal & Glow Plug Failure
problems.affectedModels: All OM601, OM602, OM603 diesel S124s.
problems.rootCause: The pre‑combustion chamber injector seals can fail, causing compression loss and hard starting — glow plugs are also a consumable item on these indirect‑injection diesels.
problems.recommendation: On a £3,000–£8,000 S124, a £200–£500 injector seal service is cheap insurance — it protects the £1,500–£4,000 engine that sits at the heart of the car.

cta.label: Compare OM60x engine prices for your S124 →

problems.title: M103/M104 Head Gasket & Oil Leak Failures
problems.rootCause: The M103 is known for head gasket and oil leak problems as mileage accumulates. The M104 suffers from the same issues, plus biodegradable wiring harness degradation on early examples.
problems.recommendation: Monitor coolant levels regularly — a £600–£1,500 head gasket replacement is dramatically cheaper than the £2,500–£5,000 engine that follows an overheat.

cta.label: Compare M103/M104 engine prices for your S124 →

problems.title: M102 Carburettor & KE‑Jetronic Issues
problems.affectedModels: Early M102 200 TE (carburettor), late M102 200 TE (KE‑Jetronic).
problems.rootCause: The Stromberg carburettor on early cars suffers from cold‑start and idle issues as it ages. The KE‑Jetronic system on later cars is more reliable but complex to diagnose.
problems.recommendation: A £300–£700 carburettor rebuild or KE‑Jetronic service is far cheaper than the £1,500–£3,500 engine that follows a lean‑running failure.

cta.label: Compare M102 engine prices for your S124 →

problems.title: S124 Load‑Bearing Rear Suspension Wear
problems.affectedModels: All S124 estates, particularly high‑mileage examples.
problems.rootCause: The S124's self‑levelling rear suspension (hydropneumatic) can develop leaks and accumulator failure — the estate's load‑bearing role puts extra stress on the system.
problems.recommendation: This is the single most common non‑engine S124 failure — have the self‑levelling suspension checked on any example over 100,000 miles.

replacementCosts.h2: S124 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are S124 estate only. W124 Saloon, C124 Coupé and A124 Cabriolet share most of these engines but are covered on their own Generation Pages, since fitment and production years vary by body style.
